---
title: "The 5-Minute Guide to Better Slides"
subtitle: "Why design systems beat design instincts"
author: "impeccable-quarto"
date: "2025-06-15"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../themes/impeccable-lightning.scss]
    slide-number: true
    transition: none
    width: 1920
    height: 1080
    center: false
    embed-resources: true
---

## The Problem

::: {.impact}
::: {.impact-number}
90%
:::
::: {.impact-label}
of slides look exactly the same — default themes, bullet walls, zero personality
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Open hard. Everyone in this room has sat through a forgettable slide deck this week.
Default Reveal.js, default PowerPoint, default Google Slides — they all blur together.
The problem isn't the tool. It's the absence of a system.
Transition: "What if typography alone could fix half of this?"
:::

---

## Typography Matters

::: {.two-col}
::: {}
### The Type Scale

A **1.25 Major Third** ratio creates clear visual hierarchy without guesswork.

- 28px body — readable from the back row
- 35px subheadings — clear separation
- 44px headings — instant focus
:::
::: {}
### Three Fonts, Three Jobs

::: {.keybox}
**Display** — Outfit / Plus Jakarta Sans

**Body** — Source Sans 3

**Code** — JetBrains Mono
:::

No font soup. No random sizes. Just a [**system**]{.hi}.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Typography is the single highest-leverage design decision.
A modular scale means every size relates mathematically to the next.
Three font families cover every use case: display for headings, body for text, mono for code.
This alone eliminates the "font soup" anti-pattern that plagues most decks.
Transition: "But what about color?"
:::

---

## Color Science

::: {.comparison}
::: {.compare-left}
### Hex Guesswork

- `#3366CC` — looks blue... maybe?
- `#FF6633` — is this accessible?
- Equal numeric steps ≠ equal visual steps
:::
::: {.compare-right}
### OKLCH Precision

- `oklch(0.45 0.18 265)` — perceptually uniform
- Lightness, chroma, hue — each axis independent
- Predictable contrast, every time
:::
:::

::: {.tipbox}
**Bonus:** Tinted neutrals replace harsh pure black and white with warm, cohesive grays.
:::

::: {.notes}
OKLCH is a perceptually uniform color space — equal numeric changes produce equal visual changes.
Hex and RGB don't have this property, which is why "picking colors that look good" is so hard.
Tinted neutrals carry a subtle hue from the primary palette, creating warmth instead of clinical harshness.
Pure black and white are penalized in our scoring system.
Transition: "Now let's talk about structure."
:::

---

## Semantic Boxes

Six containers that convey meaning, not just decoration.

::: {.two-col}
::: {}
::: {.keybox}
**Key Finding**
The one thing your audience should remember.
:::

::: {.warningbox}
**Caveat**
Limitations, risks, or things to watch out for.
:::
:::
::: {}
::: {.tipbox}
**Best Practice**
Actionable advice your audience can use today.
:::

::: {.methodbox}
**Methodology**
How you got there — process and approach.
:::
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Semantic boxes replace ad-hoc colored rectangles with purpose-built containers.
Each box type communicates intent: key findings, warnings, tips, methods, quotes, and info.
The styling comes from the theme, not inline CSS. Change the theme and every box updates.
This is the difference between a design system and decoration.
Transition: "How do we know if slides are actually good?"
:::

---

## Quality Gates

Start at **100**. Lose points for every issue detected.

| Gate | Score | What It Means |
|---|---|---|
| Failing | 0--59 | Critical problems |
| Needs Work | 60--79 | Another round needed |
| Presentable | 85--89 | Audience ready |
| [**Impeccable**]{.hi-gold} | [**95--100**]{.hi-gold} | Publication quality |

::: {.warningbox}
**Top deductions:** missing speaker notes (-5), bullet walls (-5), content overflow (-10), pure black/white (-3)
:::

::: {.notes}
Quality gates replace subjective "looks good" reviews with objective scoring.
Every presentation starts at 100 and loses points for detected issues.
The most common deductions are missing speaker notes, bullet walls, and content overflow.
A score of 90+ means the deck has been systematically reviewed and passes all major checks.
Transition: "So how do you actually start?"
:::

---

## One Command Away

```bash
# Start with a template
quarto render my-talk.qmd

# Preview with live reload
quarto preview my-talk.qmd

# Score your deck
python scripts/quality_score.py my-talk.qmd
```

::: {.keybox}
**Getting Started**

1. Pick a template from `templates/`
2. Apply the impeccable theme
3. Write content using semantic boxes
4. Render, score, iterate
:::

::: {.notes}
Three commands cover the entire workflow: render for final output, preview for iteration, and the quality scorer for automated review.
Start with a template so you get the right YAML frontmatter, then focus on content.
The design system handles visual decisions so you can focus on your message.
Transition: "Let's wrap up."
:::

---

## Better Slides Start Here {.center}

::: {.impact}
::: {.impact-statement}
Stop designing by instinct.
[Start designing by system.]{.hi}
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.tipbox}
**Try it now:** `themes/impeccable-lightning.scss` + any template from `templates/`
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.quotebox}
"Design is intelligence made visible."

[Alina Wheeler]{.attribution}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Close with the core message: systems beat instincts.
Point people to the theme file and templates directory for immediate adoption.
The quote reinforces that good design is deliberate, not decorative.
Thank the audience and invite questions if time allows.
:::
